coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot-coupled BiVO4/reduced graphene hydrogel for significant enhancement of photocatalytic activity: Antibiotic degradation and CO2 reduction
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Changchang
Xie, Zhuohong
Seo, Won Cheol
Ud Din, Syed Taj
Lee, Jeongwoo
Kim, Youjoong
Jung, Hyun
Yang, Woochul
coredata.dc:description:
In this study, we fabricated a novel hybrid photocatalyst (CD-BVO/rGH) by coupling carbon dot (CD)-decorated BiVO4 (BVO) with a reduced graphene hydrogel (rGH). The CD-BVO/rGH photocatalyst significantly enhanced the photocatalytic efficiency for tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH) degradation compared with pure BVO and BVO/rGH under simulated solar irradiation. CD-BVO/rGH also showed a substantial performance in the photocatalytic reduction of CO2. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of CD-BVO/rGH is attributed to synergistic effects, such as better light energy utilization via the upconversion effects of the CDs and the enhanced migration and separation of photoexcited carriers by the three-dimensional (3D) network structure of the rGH. The systematic analysis of the band structure, electron spin resonance, and free radical trapping revealed that h+ and
O2 – mediate the final photocatalytic degradation of pollutants. Our results provide insight into the design and construction of highly efficient hybrid photocatalysts combined with functional carbon nanostructures to enhance the photocatalytic activity for the photodegradation of environmental pollutants and CO2 reduction.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot-coupled BiVO4/reduced graphene hydrogel for significant enhancement of photocatalytic activity: Antibiotic degradation and CO2 reduction

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In this study, we fabricated a novel hybrid photocatalyst (CD-BVO/rGH) by coupling carbon dot (CD)-decorated BiVO4 (BVO) with a reduced graphene hydrogel (rGH). The CD-BVO/rGH photocatalyst significantly enhanced the photocatalytic efficiency for tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH) degradation compared with pure BVO and BVO/rGH under simulated solar irradiation. CD-BVO/rGH also showed a substantial performance in the photocatalytic reduction of CO2. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of CD-BVO/rGH is attributed to synergistic effects, such as better light energy utilization via the upconversion effects of the CDs and the enhanced migration and separation of photoexcited carriers by the three-dimensional (3D) network structure of the rGH. The systematic analysis of the band structure, electron spin resonance, and free radical trapping revealed that h+ and
O2 – mediate the final photocatalytic degradation of pollutants. Our results provide insight into the design and construction of highly efficient hybrid photocatalysts combined with functional carbon nanostructures to enhance the photocatalytic activity for the photodegradation of environmental pollutants and CO2 reduction.

ce:section.ce:para: Advances in industrialization have exacerbated the environmental challenges associated with water contamination due to the emergence of new pollutants from novel processes. These pollutants include perfluorinated chemicals, phenols, organochlorine pesticides, and antibiotics. Antibiotics are one of the most common aquatic pollutants owing to their variety, stability, and numerous applications [1–3]. Tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH) is a typical antibiotic that is widely used in human and veterinary medicine as well as in aquaculture. After this drug is metabolized in the patient’s body, the residues can enter the environment through the wastewater system, eventually damaging the ecosystem and human health [4,5]. On the other hands, global energy is predominantly provided by fossil fuels. Consumption of large quantities of fossil fuel increases CO2 emissions, which exacerbates the greenhouse effect [6]. Thus, the removal of antibiotics from the environment and the conversion of CO2 into fuels or useful chemicals are both critical challenges. Several approaches to remove antibiotics have been investigated, including adsorption, biodegradation, membrane separation, and photocatalysis. Photocatalysis is an advanced oxidative process with promising applications in the degradation of TCH in wastewater and CO2 conversion [7–9].

In contrast to other approaches for antibiotic removal, photocatalysis is powered by sunlight, which is a non-polluting, highly efficient, and convenient technology [10,11]. The key process in this technology is the conversion of solar energy into chemical energy by photocatalyst. TiO2 is one of the most used photocatalysts for the degradation of hazardous contaminants. Nevertheless, TiO2 requires ultraviolet (UV) light to be activated owing to its large band gap (3.2 eV). The limited utilization of solar energy will restrict its practical applications [12]. Therefore, the development of novel photocatalysts with substantial light utilization is of great significance.

Recently, monoclinic BiVO4 (BVO) has been extensively investigated for use as a photocatalyst because of its narrow band gap (~2.4 eV), outstanding photoresponse, and excellent stability. More significantly, compared with other semiconductors, the hybridization of Bi 6 s and O 2p orbitals in the valence band (VB) of BVO shortens the migration distance of the excited electrons in the VB of BVO to the V 3d state in the conduction band (CB) of BVO. This decreases the band gap energy [13,14]. However, the photocatalytic capacity of BVO is still unsatisfactory because of the poor separation efficiency of photoinduced electron-hole pairs, weaker surface adsorption, and relatively weak absorption of visible light [15,16].

Therefore, it is necessary to enhance the photocatalytic performance by improving the separation of photoexcited carriers, as well as boosting light absorption and surface adsorption. Previous studies have demonstrated that the use of carbon dots (CDs) significantly enhances the photocatalytic performance of semiconductor photocatalysts hybridized with CDs [17,18]. The CDs lead to a remarkable upconversion of absorbing near-infrared (NIR) light into visible light, which is absorbed by semiconductor photocatalysts. This leads to enhanced absorption and utilization of sunlight [19]. In addition, the excellent electron storage ability of CDs boosts the transport of photoinduced electrons from the semiconductors to their conducting network, thus restraining the recombination of photoinduced charge carriers [20]. Consequently, the combination of CDs and a semiconductor photocatalyst has been shown to be an effective strategy for enhancing photocatalytic performance. However, CDs have little effect on improving the surface adsorption ability of photocatalysts, suggesting the need for developing an effective approach to further enhance the surface adsorption of adsorbates for CD-decorated BVO photocatalysts.

The three-dimensional (3D) network of a composite photocatalyst composed of a 3D hydrogel and a semiconductor material has been shown to promote the surface absorptivity of semiconductors and improve the migration of photoexcited carriers [21]. Additionally, this approach allows the counterpart material to grow on the inside and outside surfaces of the 3D hydrogel network. Thus, the growth of the counterpart materials on both sides prevents material aggregation and provides more active sites. Among the various candidate hydrogels, reduced graphene hydrogel (rGH) is attractive because of its high specific surface area, outstanding conductivity, and 3D network structure, which facilitate the acceleration of migrating photoexcited carriers, dispersing the photocatalyst, and adsorbing target molecules. Furthermore, the 3D hydrogel network structure provides multidimensional channels for mass and electron transfer during the catalytic reaction processes. These advantages enhance photocatalytic performance [22].

In this study, a novel CD-decorated BVO/rGH hybrid photocatalyst was successfully fabricated via in situ solvothermal growth. The combination of CD-decorated BVO with rGH distinctly improved the physical and chemical properties of the photocatalyst. The results of the photocatalytic performance tests revealed that CD-BVO/rGH significantly improved the photocatalytic activity for removing TCH and reducing CO2. Finally, a possible photocatalytic reaction mechanism is suggested in terms of the separation and migration behaviors of the photoinduced carriers, as well as the formation of active species in the CD-BVO/rGH composite. This research presents the possibility of improving photocatalytic efficiency by coupling semiconductor photocatalysts with multifunctional materials that have excellent conductivity and high specific surface area.

Graphite, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO), isopropanol (IPA), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt dihydrate (EDTA-2Na), benzoquinone (BQ), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), ascorbic acid, glucose, hydrochloric acid (HCl), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), nitric acid (HNO3), ethylene glycol, ammonium metavanadate (NH4VO3), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), ethanol, triethanolamine (TEOA), sodium sulphate (Na2SO4) and urea (CO(NH2)2) were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Beijing Cl., Ltd (Shanghai, China). TCH was provided by Jiangsu Shengjie Mao Biotechnology Co., Ltd. All the reagents were analytical grade and used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of graphite oxide (GO) and rGH
ce:section.ce:para: GO was prepared using a modified Hummers’ method [23]. The method involved dispersion of 3 g of graphite (325 mesh) into 70 mL of H2SO4 in an ice bath followed by stirring for 10 min and addition of 1.5 g of NaNO3 and 9 g of KMnO4. The mixture was cooled down to below 20 °C and stirred for 3 h. The temperature was then increased to 35 °C and maintained for 4 h, followed by the addition of 150 mL of deionized (DI) water to the solution. The solution was then heated to 95 °C and stirred for 2 h. Next, 300 mL of DI water and 20 mL of H2O2 (30 wt%) were gradually added. The products were washed and centrifuged with HCl (10 wt%), and dialyzed against water for 7 days to obtain GO. To prepare rGH structures, the mixture of 50 mg of GO and 0.5 g of ascorbic acid was ultrasonicated for 5 h and stirred for 0.5 h, followed by heating at 95 °C for 1 h. The rGH was obtained after washing the products with DI water five times and freeze-drying for 48 h.

CDs were synthesized using glucose as the carbon source. Typically, 9 g of glucose and 0.8 g of NaOH were dissolved in 50 mL of DI water, followed by vigorous stirring for 2 h and ultrasonication for 1.5 h. When the solution turned brown, the pH of the solution was adjusted to 7 using HCl (1 mol/L). Next, the brown solution was further dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3500 Da) for 48 h, to obtain a stable CDs suspension.

A series of CD-BVO/rGH was synthesized using a one-step in situ growth method. Then, 4 mmol of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 0.02 g of rGH were added to 10 mL of HNO3 (1 mol/L) and followed by ultrasonication for 0.5 h and stirring for 2 h. Next, 6 mmol of NH4VO3 and 50 mmol of CO(NH2)2 were slowly added to the mixture and stirred for 0.5 h. Subsequently, different volumes of CD suspensions (1, 3, 5, 7, and 9 mL denoted as CD-BVO/rGH-1, CD-BVO/rGH-3, CD-BVO/rGH-5, CD-BVO/rGH-7, CD-BVO/rGH-9, respectively) were added slowly to the mixtures and continuously stirred. The solvent was evaporated at 85 °C. The synthesized products were washed with DI water and ethanol three times and dried for 24 h to obtain the final CD-BVO/rGH. BVO and BVO/rGH were synthesized under the same conditions for comparison with CD-BVO/rGH.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using a D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) at room temperature. All XRD patterns were obtained from 10° to 80° at a scan rate of 7°/min. Raman spectra were obtained in the range of 200–1700 cm−1 at room temperature using a Thermo Fisher Scientific DXR Raman spectrometer (Waltham, USA) equipped with a light source (wavelength of 532 nm and power of 1 mW). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) measurements were performed via a field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, JSM-7001F, JEOL) at an accelerating voltage of 0.5–30 kV. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) measurements were conducted using a JEM 2100 microscope at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a PHI5300 (Perkin Elmer) with an Al Kα (1486.6 eV) monochromatic X-ray source. The binding energy was referenced to the C 1s line at 284.6 eV for calibration. Surface charging was compensated using a neutralizing system in which a high current density flood gun with a narrow energy spread (to reduce potential variation on the sample) is used in combination with a source of low-energy positive ions (to neutralize the negative potential in the peripheral region). For XPS measurement, about 80 mg of the sample was pressed into a pellet (diameter of 7–8 mm and thickness of 0.2–0.4 nm). This pellet was mounted on a sample holder with a piece of conductive glue. The spectrometer was operated in the analyzer energy mode with a pass energy of 100 eV for wide-range survey spectra and 50 eV for region XPS spectra. The work functions of rGH and CDs were measured using ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) (Nexsa, Thermofisher Sci.) with a He I source (excitation energy of 21.2 eV). To observe the cutoff regions of secondary electrons in the UPS spectra, a constant pass energy of 1 eV and a bias voltage of −5 eV were applied to the samples during the UPS measurement. Before UPS measurement, the samples were cleaned in situ with Ar ion sputtering at 2 kV for 30 s. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (QuantaMaster 40) equipped with a 75 W Xe lamp at room temperature in air under an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. UV–vis absorption spectra of the samples were obtained using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV2450) equipped with an integrating sphere. BaSO4 was used as the reflectance standard. Total organic carbon (TOC) was measured using a multi N/C 2100 (Analytik Jena AG, Germany) TOC analyzer. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were recorded on an A300-10/12 ESR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). A 500 W high-pressure mercury lamp equipped with an ultraviolet cutoff filter (λ ≥ 420 nm) provided visible light at room temperature. Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms were obtained under N2 on a NOVA-2000 instrument to obtain the pore sizes of the samples using the Barret–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) method.

The photocatalytic performance of the samples was assessed via the degradation of TCH under UV–Vis light irradiation (300 W Xe lamp). Photocatalyst powders (0.05 g) were dispersed in a 20 mg/L TCH aqueous solution (100 mL). Before light irradiation, the mixed solution was stirred in the dark for 0.5 h to bring the solution to adsorption–desorption equilibrium. After the light source was turned on, an aliquot solution of 4 mL was taken every 20 min, and the photocatalytic particles were removed via filtration. The light absorbance of the test solution was determined using a UV spectrophotometer at a wavelength of 357 nm.

The CO2 reduction was performed by placing 0.05 g of samples on fiberglass sheets inside the reactor. Pure CO2 (99.999%) gas was bubbled into the reactor containing 90 mL of water and 10 mL of TEOA for 30 min to remove gas impurities from the reactor. Next, the pressure of the photochemical reactor was maintained at 0.5 MPa. The gas products were tested via gas chromatography (GC5890, FID detector with reformer, Nanjing Kejie instrument) at periodic intervals.

Transient photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out using an electrochemical workstation (CHI 852C, Chenhua Instruments Co.) in a standard three-electrode system using the as-prepared samples as the working electrode, a saturated Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode, and a Pt plate as the counter electrode. A Na2SO4 solution (0.5 M) was employed as the electrolyte.

The crystalline structures of rGH, BVO, BVO/rGH and CD-BVO/rGH were analyzed via XRD measurements, as shown in Fig. 1 a. A broad and wide peak in rGH was detected at 24.9°, which can be described as (002) reflections, implying interlayer peeling following the reduction of GO and the formation of a thinner layer [24]. Surprisingly, the BVO, BVO/rGH and CD-BVO/rGH present similar diffraction patterns that correspond to the monoclinic scheelite phase of BVO (PDF #14-0688) [25]. The absence of diffraction peaks belonging to rGH or the CDs within the CD-BVO/rGH can probably be attributed to the high dispersion of CDs as well as the low crystallinity of rGH and CDs [19,26]. This reveals that the introduction of rGH and CDs barely affects the crystal structure of BVO.

Raman spectra were also analyzed to understand the interactions between the CDs, BVO/rGH and CD-BVO/rGH (Fig. 1b). Raman peaks appear at 208, 328, 365, and 823 cm−1 in all samples, corresponding to the typical vibrational bands of BVO [15]. In the Raman spectra of BVO/rGH and CD-BVO/rGH, peaks located at 1345 cm−1 and 1594 cm−1 are described as the D-band (disordered sp2 carbon atoms) and G band (conjugated sp2 carbon atoms), respectively [20]. The intensity of the D-band and G-band in CD-BVO/rGH was higher than that of BVO/rGH, which could be attributed to the introduction of the CDs. The typical bands of BVO and carbon were detected in the Raman spectrum of CD-BVO/rGH, implying successful coupling of BVO, CDs, and rGH. These results were consistent with the XRD results.

The morphology and microstructure of the rGH, CDs, BVO/rGH and CD-BVO/rGH were examined using SEM, TEM, and HRTEM. The SEM image of rGH exhibits a poly porous 3D network structure consisting of freely arrayed graphene sheets (Fig. 2 a). The formation of the network structure is based on hydrophobicity, π-π conjugation, and intramolecular forces between the graphene sheets [27]. As shown in Fig. 2b and c, the BVO/rGH and CD-BVO/rGH display similar morphologies, while a few nanoparticles were grown in the network and distributed on both sides of the graphene sheets. The TEM image of the CD suspension in Fig. 2d shows that the small black dots represent the CDs. They are practically monodispersed without obvious aggregation, with an average size of 1–5 nm. In Fig. 2e, the lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.309 nm correspond to the (121) planes of monoclinic BVO. The other lattice fringe was calculated to have a spacing of 0.333 nm, which is attributed to the (002) plane of the CDs. Furthermore, the HAADF and elemental mapping images of CD-BVO/rGH in Fig. 2f-j demonstrate that the distribution of the C, Bi, V, and O elements are reasonable and consistent with the SEM and HRTEM images. These results further verify the growth of BVO nanoparticles in the rGH network and the growth of CDs on the surface of the BVO nanoparticles.

The surface composition of CD-BVO/rGH was determined using XPS. The survey XPS spectrum shows the co-existence of chemical elements O, V, C and Bi on the surface of CD-BVO/rGH (Fig. S1). In the Bi 4f XPS spectrum of CD-BVO/rGH, two peaks at binding energies of 164. 6 and 159.3 eV correspond to Bi3+ 4f5/2 and Bi3+ 4f7/2, respectively (Fig. 3 a) [28]. The V 2p spectrum was fitted by two peaks centered at 524.3 eV and 517.0 eV, which belong to the V 2p1/2 and V 2p3/2 in the CD-BVO/rGH, respectively (Fig. 3b) [29]. The deconvoluted C 1s spectrum of CD-BVO/rGH (Fig. 3c) exhibits three peaks centered at 284.6, 285.7, and 289.7 eV, which were described as CC, CO, and CO species, respectively [30]. The O 1s spectrum was divided into two peaks at 532.5 and 530.2 eV (Fig. 3d). The peak at 532.5 eV corresponds to the OH bond originating from the adsorbed H2O or OH (Oabs), while the peak at 530.2 eV might belong to the BiO bond (Olat) [31]. These results further confirmed the existence of BVO, CDs and rGH. Thus, XRD, Raman, SEM, TEM, and XPS measurements of the synthesized composites confirm the successful formation of CD-decorated BVO coupled with rGH.

The photocatalytic activity of pure BVO, BVO/rGH, and CD-BVO/rGH with various amounts of CDs (CD-BVO/rGH-1, -3, -5, -7, and -9) was investigated via degradation of TCH under light irradiation from a 300 W Xe lamp, as shown in Fig. 4 a. The results demonstrate that the photocatalytic activity of CD-BVO/rGH ternary composites was significantly enhanced in comparison to that of the binary BVO/rGH composites (58.8%) and pure BVO (33.6%). In addition, the enhanced photocatalytic efficiency of the CD-BVO/rGH composite was associated with the amounts of decorated CDs. As the amounts of decorated CDs in CD-BVO/rGH increased, the photocatalytic efficiency increased from 58.8% for pure BVO/rGH to 80.1% for CD-BVO/rGH-7. Nevertheless, as the amount of decorated CDs in CD-BVO/rGH is greater than that in CD-BVO/rGH-7, the photocatalytic efficiency begins to decline, which may be attributed to the formation of an insulating layer of CDs due to CD aggregation on the BVO surface, which interrupts the incident light and results in fewer active sites [32].

The degradation kinetics of TCH were analyzed to determine the reaction rates of the various photocatalysts. The reaction rate was obtained using the first-order kinetic equation:
ln
C 0
/ C
= k t
, where C0 , C, and k refer to the TCH concentration after adsorption in the dark, TCH concentration at irradiation time, and the reaction rate constant, respectively (Fig. 4b) [33]. The fitting results indicate that the k values of BVO, BVO/rGH, CD-BVO/rGH-1, CD-BVO/rGH-3, CD-BVO/rGH-5, CD-BVO/rGH-7, and CD-BVO/rGH-9 were 0.0034, 0.0075, 0.0092, 0.0098, 0.0107, 0.0121 and 0.0112 min−1, respectively. The k value of CD-BVO/rGH-7 was 3.56-fold and 1.61-fold higher than that of BVO and BVO/rGH, respectively.

TOC was employed to determine the level of mineralization in the TCH solution, as shown in Fig. 5 a. The mineralization rate of CD-BVO/rGH-7 was 74.9%, which was 2.9-fold and 1.5-fold higher than that of BVO and BVO/rGH, respectively. In addition, the stability of a photocatalyst is a significant property for practical applications. The stability of CD-BVO/rGH-7 was evaluated by cyclic degradation tests with a TCH solution, as shown in Fig. 5b. No apparent decline in degradation efficiency was detected after 4 cycles, demonstrating the outstanding stability of CD-BVO/rGH-7 for TCH degradation [34]. The XRD patterns of CD-BVO/rGH-7 before and after four cycles were compared to confirm their stability, as shown in Fig. 5c. No significant difference was observed in the characteristic peaks, further proving the excellent stability of CD-BVO/rGH-7 [35].

We also conducted a photocatalytic performance test on CO2 reduction over CD-BVO/rGH-7, as shown in Fig. S2a. The yields of CH4 and CO reduced from CO2 were 32.2 µmol g−1 and 92.3 µmol g−1 within 180 min, respectively. In addition, the yields of CH4 and CO were maintained after six cyclic tests (Fig. S2 b). In conclusion, the CD-BVO/rGH composite exhibited outstanding photocatalytic performance in TCH degradation and satisfactory performance in the photocatalytic reduction of CO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of factors underlying improved photocatalytic activity
ce:section.ce:para: The Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the materials directly reflect the recombination rates of photoexcited charge carriers [36]. Thus, we performed PL measurements for BVO, BVO/rGH and CD-BVO/rGH to evaluate their recombination efficiency (Fig. 6 a). The PL intensity for the CD-BVO/rGH was notably reduced compared to that of BVO and BVO/rGH. The fluorescence quenching via interconnection of the CDs and rGH may be attributed to the enhanced transfer of photoexcited electrons at the interface between BVO and rGH/CD, which promotes the separation of photoexcited electrons and holes, resulting in improved photocatalytic performance [37]. In addition, the UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra were measured to determine the light absorption efficiency of BVO, BVO/rGH, and CD-BVO/rGH (Fig. 6b). The light absorption edges of BVO and BVO/rGH were similar at ~540 nm. However, the absorbance of BVO/rGH significantly increased at wavelengths greater than 540 nm compared with that of BVO. For CD-BVO/rGH, the absorption edge was distinctly red-shifted from 540 nm to 590 nm. Moreover, the absorption intensity of light in the visible to infrared wavelength range was further increased in contrast to BVO/rGH. The extended wavelength of light absorption results from the upconversion effect of the CDs introduced into BVO/rGH. The migration efficiency of the photoexcited carriers at the interface of the photocatalytic system was explored using EIS (Fig. 6c). The smaller arc radius in the Nyquist plots suggests faster interfacial migration and more effective separation of the photoexcited carriers [38]. Notably, the arc radius of BVO/rGH is smaller than that of BVO, implying a smaller resistance to carrier migration [39]. Thus, the photoexcited electrons can migrate rapidly from BVO to rGH, resulting in the inhibition of recombination of photoexcited carriers. Particularly, CD-BVO/rGH exhibits the smallest arc radius, which may lead to electron capture by the CDs, revealing that the migration resistance at the surface/interface of the photocatalytic systems is further reduced [40]. The photocurrent responses of BVO, BVO/rGH, and CD-BVO/rGH are shown in Fig. 6d. CD-BVO/rGH displayed the highest photocurrent density under visible light irradiation. It is approximately 1.3-fold of BVO/rGH and 1.76-fold of BVO, indicating that CD-BVO/rGH provides outstanding separation efficiency of photoexcited charges [41]. The results also verify that rGH offers a rapid and multidimensional path for the migration of photoexcited electrons, while the CDs serve as electron cages and vehicles. Thus, CD-BVO/rGH exhibited excellent charge separation and migration capabilities. Moreover, to evaluate the relative active sites of each sample for the photocatalytic reaction, the surface areas of BVO and CD-BVO/rGH were investigated by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms, as shown in Fig. S3 and Table S1. The isotherms of both samples can be identified as typical type IV curves with H4 hysteresis loops in the P/P0 range of 0.4–1.0, which are characteristic of mesoporous structures. The specific surface areas of BVO and CD-BVO/rGH-7 were calculated to be 3.46 and 9.03 m2 g−1, respectively. Apparently, the composite of rGH and BVO can effectively provide more reaction sites.

To establish the photocatalytic reaction mechanism of the CD-BVO/rGH composite during TCH degradation and CO2 reduction, we analyzed the optical band structure and the energy band edge of the composite, and reactive radicals during the photocatalytic processes.

The band gap of BVO (Fig. 7 a) was assessed using a Tauc plot [42], based on Eq. (1):
(1)
α h ν = A
h ν -
E g
1 / n
where Eg, h, ν and α denote the band gap, Planck’s constant, optical frequency, and absorption coefficient, respectively. For BVO, n is 2 because of the indirect band gap semiconductor [43]. Based on the tangential line connecting the plots, the Eg value of BVO was estimated to be 2.44 eV. The VB maximum (EVB) of BVO was obtained to be 0.57 eV from the XPS valence band spectrum of BVO (Fig. 7b). Consequently, the calculated value of the CB minimum (ECB) of BVO was −1.87 eV. The work functions of rGH and CDs were also obtained from UPS measurements [44,45]. Fig. 7c and d show the UPS spectra of rGH and CDs in the secondary electron cutoff region, respectively. Linear extrapolation was used to determine the positions of the secondary electron cutoff (ESECO). By measuring the energy width (ESECO) of the emitted electrons from the onset of the secondary electrons up to the Fermi edge and using Eq. (2), the work function (φ) can be given by the following formula [46]:
(2)
φ = h ν -
E
SECO
where hν is 21.2 eV, which is the energy of the incident UV light (He I). Thus, the work functions of rGH and CDs are calculated to be 3.4 and 4.3 eV, respectively. Thus, the Fermi Levels (EF) of rGH and CDs are −1.1 and −0.2 eV (vs. NHE), respectively.

To identify the active substances during the degradation of TCH, trapping experiments were conducted using various quenchers in the degradation system, as shown in Fig. 7e. The introduction of IPA as an
OH scavenger led to only a slight decrease in the degradation rate of TCH compared to the system without quenchers. In comparison, the introduction of EDTA-2Na as a h+ scavenger and BQ as an
O2 – scavenger led to a significant decrease in the degradation rate of TCH to 84% and 95%, respectively. Therefore, we can conclude that h+ and
O2 – play significant roles in the degradation of TCH [47,48]. ESR spectroscopy was also employed to further identify the state of the free radicals formed on the photocatalyst surface under light illumination. Four characteristic signals of DMPO-
O2 – were obtained under irradiation in the presence of CD-BVO/rGH-7 (Fig. 7f). However, no signals were observed in the dark. The results indicate that
O2 – was produced on the surface of CD-BVO/rGH-7, and light irradiation was indispensable for producing
O2 – [49].

Based on the above analysis of the optical response, the band structure, the migration behavior of photoexcited charge carriers, and the involved active substances, we propose a possible mechanism for the photocatalytic degradation of TCH and the reduction of CO2 over the CD-BVO/rGH composite photocatalyst, as shown in Fig. 8 (Part Ⅰ) and (Part Ⅱ). Exposure of CD-BVO/rGH to sunlight leads to absorption of NIR light by the CDs and conversion to visible light due to the light upconversion effect. The converted visible light excites the BVO to produce photoinduced charges. Meanwhile, BVO is directly excited by visible light to generate photoinduced charges. Thus, NIR and visible light induced photoexcited charge carriers on the CD-decorated BVO.

The excellent conductivity and large π conjugation of the 3D-networked rGH strongly facilitate electron acceptance and provide multidimensional pathways for the rapid migration of photoinduced electrons, while the CDs exhibit a strong ability to capture electrons as electron cages. In addition, the ECB of BVO (-1.87 eV) is more negative than the EF of rGH (-1.1 eV) and CDs (-0.2 eV). Therefore, the photoexcited electrons on the ECB of BVO rapidly migrate to the rGH and CD surfaces [50].

The migrated electrons on the surface of the rGH and CDs reduce O2 to
O2 –, which is followed by the degradation of TCH by
O2 – to small molecules. Meanwhile, the photoinduced holes in the EVB of BVO cannot oxidize H2O to
OH because the potential of –OH/
OH (1.99 eV) is more positive than that of EVB of BVO (0.57 eV) [51]. Thus, the photoinduced holes directly degrade the TCH to small molecules.

The mechanism for CO2 reduction was similar (Part II). Compared with THC degradation, the photoexcited electrons are transferred from the ECB of BVO to the surfaces of the CDs and rGH, where they then reduce CO2 to CO and CH4. Meanwhile, the photoinduced holes in the EVB of BVO are consumed by TEOA as sacrificial electron donors [52]. The reaction steps for the reduction of CO2 are as follows:

Consequently, coupling CDs and rGH can effectively promote the separation and migration of photoinduced carriers and offer additional active sites for chemical reactions. This results in the enhanced photocatalytic performance of the hybrid photocatalyst.

In this work, we synthesized a CD-BVO/rGH composite photocatalyst by coupling CD-decorated BVO with rGH. The CD-decorated BVO particles were uniformly distributed in the rGH network. The efficiency and rate of TCH degradation mediated by CD-BVO/rGH with 7 mL of CD suspension were nearly 2.4-fold and 3.6-fold higher than that of BVO/rGH and BVO, respectively. In addition, CD-BVO/rGH also exhibited a substantial photocatalytic reduction of CO2 with CH4 and CO yields of 32.2 µmol g−1 and 92.3 µmol g−1, respectively, within 180 min. The directional migration of charge carriers from the BVO to the CDs and rGH significantly accelerated the transfer and separation of the photogenerated charge carriers. Thus, the CDs and 3D network structure of CD-BVO/rGH offer additional active sites, resulting in enhanced photocatalytic performance. Furthermore, the trapping experiment revealed that
O2 – and h+ were the main active species. This study paves the way for the construction of hybrid photocatalysts decorated with functional carbon nanostructures to enhance the photocatalytic activity for the photodegradation of environmental pollutants and the reduction of CO2 to fuels.
